learn.title: Types of Solar Panels Compared
learn.intro: Understanding solar panel types helps you choose the right technology for rooftop installations. Each panel type offers different trade-offs between efficiency, cost, and installation flexibility, making this knowledge crucial for the renewable energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Monocrystalline panels use single-crystal silicon for 20-22% efficiency but cost more. Polycrystalline panels have multiple crystals at 15-17% efficiency and are cheaper. Thin-film panels are lightweight at 10-12% efficiency but flexible for curved surfaces.
learn.c2_text: Higher efficiency panels produce more power per square meter, ideal for limited roof space. Lower efficiency panels work better where space is abundant. Temperature coefficient also varies - monocrystalline performs better in hot climates than polycrystalline.
learn.c3_text: India's solar ambitions rely heavily on cost-effective polycrystalline panels for its massive rooftop programs. The National Solar Mission targets 100 GW non-residential solar by 2022, with domestic manufacturing of polycrystalline technology under production-linked incentives.
learn.stat1: Global solar capacity reached 1.4 TW by end-2023
learn.stat3: Thin-film technology holds 5% of global PV market share
learn.stat4: India installed 17.5 GW solar capacity by March 2024

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels Explained
learn.intro: Bifacial panels capture sunlight on both sides, boosting energy output without additional roof space. This innovative technology maximizes power generation in utility-scale projects, accelerating the economics of large solar installations worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels generate power from both front and back sides using albedo reflection - typically 10-30% more energy than standard panels. They work best in snowy regions or areas with high ground reflectivity like white-roofed warehouses or sandy deserts.
learn.c2_text: Mounting height and ground cover affect back-side generation. Panels mounted 1-2 meters above ground with light-colored surfaces underneath can increase total yield by 15-25%. Tracking systems further optimize both sides throughout the day.
learn.c3_text: India's solar parks increasingly adopt bifacial panels for higher returns. NTPC's 2024 tenders include bifacial technology for 500 MW projects in Rajasthan and Gujarat, where high albedo from desert sand boosts back-side generation by 15-20% annually.
learn.stat1: Bifacial panels produce 10-30% more energy than mono-facial panels
learn.stat2: Desert installations see 20% annual gain from back-side generation
learn.stat3: Global bifacial market grew 45% year-over-year in 2023
learn.stat4: India's solar park scheme includes 25% bifacial panel allocation

learn.title: Capacity Factor Explained
learn.intro: Capacity factor reveals the difference between a power plant's maximum potential and its actual energy production. This metric is essential for understanding grid reliability and comparing different renewable energy technologies fairly.
learn.c1_text: Capacity factor measures actual output divided by maximum possible output over time. A plant with 50% capacity factor produces half its rated power continuously. Wind farms typically run 25-50%, while solar farms achieve 15-25% due to day-night cycles and weather.
learn.c2_text: Grid operators use capacity factors to predict reliable power availability. A 100 MW solar farm with 20% capacity factor provides 20 MW average output. This affects investment decisions, grid stability planning, and backup power requirements during low-production periods.
learn.c3_text: India's solar capacity factor averages 22% nationwide, but drops to 15% in monsoon regions. The 2024 Grid Stability Plan requires 120 GW renewable capacity to meet 100 GW peak demand, factoring in seasonal capacity variations across states like Tamil Nadu and Punjab.
learn.stat1: Global average solar capacity factor ranges 15-25%
learn.stat2: Wind farms typically achieve 25-50% capacity factor
learn.stat3: India's solar capacity factor averages 22% nationally
learn.stat4: Coal plants maintain 80-90% capacity factor consistently
learn.readLabel: Explore at US Energy Info Admin

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage
learn.intro: Pumped hydro storage acts as the world's largest battery, storing excess electricity by pumping water uphill and releasing it through turbines when needed. This proven technology provides crucial grid stability for renewable energy integration globally.
learn.c1_text: Pumped hydro uses two reservoirs at different elevations. During excess electricity, pumps move water uphill to the upper reservoir. When demand is high, water flows downhill through turbines to generate electricity. Efficiency reaches 70-80%, making it the most deployed storage technology.
learn.c2_text: A typical pumped hydro facility stores 1-30 GWh of energy. The global fleet provides over 900 GW of storage capacity - more than all other storage technologies combined. Each facility can power hundreds of thousands of homes for hours or days depending on discharge rate.
learn.c3_text: India has identified 266 pumped hydro sites with 96 GW potential capacity. The 2024 National Hydro Energy Policy prioritizes 30 GW pumped hydro by 2030. Major projects include the 1200 MW Koel-Karjah Hydro Pumping Station in Jharkhand, supporting grid stability for renewable energy integration.
learn.stat1: Global pumped hydro provides 900 GW storage capacity
learn.stat3: India identified 266 sites for pumped hydro development
learn.stat4: Typical facilities store 1-30 GWh of energy

learn.title: Lithium-ion Battery Storage
learn.intro: Lithium-ion batteries power our portable devices and increasingly stabilize electrical grids. Their fast response and declining costs make them essential for smoothing renewable energy intermittency and enabling electric vehicle adoption worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Lithium-ion batteries move lithium ions between carbon anode and metal oxide cathode through an electrolyte. During discharge, ions flow from anode to cathode, generating electricity. These rechargeable cells connect in modules to form battery systems for homes, vehicles, and grid-scale applications.
learn.c2_text: Battery storage provides frequency regulation within seconds, peak shaving during high demand, and renewable smoothing. A 100 MW/200 MWh battery can supply 100 MW for 2 hours. Grid-scale systems now cost $150-300/kWh, down 80% since 2010, enabling widespread deployment.
learn.c3_text: India's 2024 Production-Linked Incentive scheme offers ₹1,500 crore for 50 GWh battery manufacturing. The 500 MW Atal Bhujal Yojana includes battery storage for solar pumps. NTPC plans 2.5 GWh grid-scale battery deployment by 2025, supporting India's target of 500 MW storage capacity by 2030.
learn.stat2: Battery storage response time is under 1 second
learn.stat3: India allocated ₹1,500 crore for 50 GWh battery manufacturing
learn.stat4: Global battery storage deployed reached 26 GW in 2023

learn.title: Building Integrated PV Systems
learn.intro: Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) replace traditional building materials with solar-generating elements. This dual-function approach transforms walls, roofs, and facades into power generators, revolutionizing sustainable architecture and urban energy systems.
learn.c1_text: BIPV integrates solar cells directly into building components like roof tiles, facade panels, and windows. Unlike traditional panels mounted on existing structures, BIPV serves as both weatherproofing material and electricity generator. Solar windows use transparent photovoltaics while maintaining visibility and aesthetics.
learn.c2_text: BIPV systems typically produce 100-200 W per square meter, lower than rooftop panels due to integration constraints. Architectural flexibility allows curved surfaces and custom shapes. Modern BIPV can match any building aesthetic while delivering 50-100 kWh per square meter annually depending on location and orientation.
learn.c3_text: Delhi's 2024 Solar Mission mandates BIPV for new government buildings. Mumbai's coastal development plans include BIPV facades for typhoon resistance. The 2025 Smart Cities Mission allocates ₹500 crore for BIPV integration in 500 public buildings, targeting 25 MW rooftop solar generation through architectural innovation.
learn.stat1: BIPV produces 100-200 W per square meter
learn.stat2: Global BIPV market reached $12.8 billion in 2023
learn.stat3: Delhi mandates BIPV for new government constructions
learn.stat4: Mumbai plans 25 MW BIPV generation by 2025
